title: Astrophotography Editing: Stacking and Processing Basics
excerpt: Why your single frame looks nothing like Instagram and how to fix it. Learn the fundamentals of stacking, calibration frames, and post-processing.
author: Ace Stellar
body:
## The Dirty Secret of Astrophotography

Here's something experienced astrophotographers don't always mention: a single raw frame from the camera looks terrible. Noisy, dark, flat, nothing like the vibrant Milky Way photos you see online. That's not because their cameras are better than yours — it's because processing is half the craft.

If you've been disappointed comparing your raw shots to finished images online, relax. Everyone's raw shots look like that. The magic is in the editing. Let's demystify it.

## Why Single Frames Look Bad

Three main reasons:

**1. Noise.** High ISO amplifies the signal from stars and the Milky Way, but it also amplifies random electronic noise from your sensor. At ISO 3200-6400, noise is very visible — random colored speckles that obscure detail.

**2. Dynamic range.** Your camera captures a flat, low-contrast image by design (especially in RAW). This preserves the most data for editing but looks bland straight out of camera.

**3. Light pollution gradients.** Even at dark sites, there's some sky glow that creates uneven brightness across your image — brighter near the horizon, darker overhead.

All three of these problems are fixable in post-processing.

## Stacking: The Free Quality Upgrade

Stacking is the most powerful technique in astrophotography processing. The concept is simple: take many identical exposures (called "sub-frames" or "subs") and average them together.

**Why it works:** Noise is random. In frame 1, a noisy pixel might be red. In frame 2, the same pixel might be blue. In frame 3, it might be green. When you average all three, the random noise cancels out, but the actual star light (which is consistent across all frames) remains.

**The math:** Stacking N frames reduces noise by the square root of N.
- 4 frames = 2x noise reduction
- 16 frames = 4x noise reduction
- 64 frames = 8x noise reduction
- 100 frames = 10x noise reduction

This means 16 stacked frames are equivalent to a camera with 4x better noise performance. That's like upgrading from an entry-level crop sensor to a medium format sensor — for free.

## How to Stack: Nightscape (Milky Way + Foreground)

For widefield Milky Way shots where you want sharp stars AND a sharp foreground:

**Software:** Sequator (free, Windows) or Starry Landscape Stacker (Mac, $40)

**Workflow:**
1. Shoot 10-20 identical frames (same composition, same settings) plus one frame where you light-paint or expose for the foreground.
2. Import all frames into Sequator.
3. Select one frame as the "base" image.
4. Draw a "ground region" mask to tell the software which part is the landscape.
5. Click "Start." The software aligns the stars across all frames (compensating for Earth's rotation between shots), stacks the sky, and keeps the foreground sharp from your base frame.
6. Output is a single TIFF with dramatically less noise in the sky.

The result is stunning. What was a noisy mess becomes a clean, detailed image.

## How to Stack: Deep Sky Objects

For tracked deep sky images (nebulae, galaxies):

**Software:** DeepSkyStacker (free, Windows), Siril (free, cross-platform), or PixInsight ($230)

**Workflow:**
1. Shoot 30-200+ light frames (your actual exposures of the target).
2. Shoot calibration frames (explained below).
3. Load everything into your stacking software.
4. The software registers (aligns), calibrates, and stacks all frames.
5. Output is a master image ready for further processing.

## Calibration Frames: What They Are and Why They Matter

Calibration frames remove systematic errors from your images. There are three types:

**Dark frames:** Exposures taken with the lens cap ON, same settings and temperature as your light frames. They capture the sensor's thermal noise pattern, which the software subtracts from your lights.
- Shoot 20-30 darks at the end of your session.
- Same ISO, same shutter speed, same temperature.

**Flat frames:** Evenly illuminated exposures (point the camera at a white t-shirt over the lens with a flashlight behind it, or photograph the blue twilight sky). They capture vignetting (corner darkening) and dust spots on your sensor.
- Shoot 20-30 flats the next morning.
- Same focal length and focus position as your lights. Use Aperture Priority mode.

**Bias frames (or offset frames):** The shortest possible exposure with the lens cap on. They capture the sensor's read noise pattern.
- Shoot 50+ bias frames at any time.
- Same ISO as your lights, fastest shutter speed (1/8000 or whatever your camera's max is).

**Do you always need calibration frames?** For casual nightscape stacking, you can skip them and still get good results. For serious deep sky work, they make a noticeable difference. Start without them, then add them as you get more experienced.

## Post-Processing a Milky Way Image

Here's a basic Lightroom/Camera Raw workflow for a stacked Milky Way image:

**1. White balance:** Start around 3800-4000K. Adjust until the Milky Way shows warm golden tones in the core and cool blue tones in the surrounding sky. This is subjective — find what looks natural to you.

**2. Exposure:** Bump up +0.3 to +0.7 if the image is dark. Don't overdo it.

**3. Contrast:** Increase to +20-40. This separates the Milky Way from the background sky.

**4. Highlights:** Pull down to -30 to -60. Recovers bright star detail.

**5. Shadows:** Boost +30 to +60. Reveals fainter Milky Way structure and foreground detail.

**6. Whites:** Slight boost +10 to +20. Makes stars pop.

**7. Blacks:** Pull down -10 to -30. Deepens the dark sky for more contrast.

**8. Clarity / Texture:** Clarity +20-40, Texture +10-20. Enhances the Milky Way's structure. Don't overdo it — too much clarity looks crunchy.

**9. Dehaze:** +10-20. Cuts through any residual atmospheric haze or light pollution.

**10. Vibrance:** +15-30. Brings out the natural colors of the Milky Way (reds, purples, golds) without oversaturating.

**11. Saturation:** +5-10 at most. A little goes a long way. Over-saturated Milky Way photos are the #1 sign of over-processing.

**12. Noise reduction:** If you stacked, you'll need less. Luminance NR 10-20. Detail slider at 50+.

**13. Graduated filter:** Drag from the horizon up, reduce exposure -0.3 to -0.5 to darken the light-pollution glow on the horizon.

**14. Lens corrections:** Enable profile corrections to fix vignetting and distortion.

## Common Editing Mistakes

- **Over-saturation** — The Milky Way should not look like a neon sign. Subtle color is more impressive than screaming color.
- **Over-sharpening** — Creates ugly halos around stars. Use gentle sharpening or skip it entirely for stacked images.
- **Nuclear clarity** — Clarity at +100 makes everything look like a bad HDR photo. Stay under +40.
- **Neon green foreground** — This happens when you push shadows and saturation together. Desaturate greens or reduce overall saturation.
- **Fake-looking composites** — If you blend sky and foreground, match the lighting direction and color temperature. The Moon can't be in the sky AND casting no shadows on the ground.
- **Star removal** — Removing stars with AI tools is trendy but controversial. It can look amazing or very artificial.

## Free Software Recommendations

- **Sequator** — Best free nightscape stacker (Windows)
- **DeepSkyStacker** — Best free deep sky stacker (Windows)
- **Siril** — Complete free deep sky processing pipeline (Windows/Mac/Linux)
- **GIMP** — Free Photoshop alternative for compositing and final touches
- **RawTherapee / Darktable** — Free Lightroom alternatives for RAW processing
- **StarStaX** — Free star trail stacking (all platforms)
- **AutoStakkert** — Free planetary/lunar stacking
- **Registax** — Free wavelet sharpening for planetary/lunar

## Before and After: What to Expect

Set realistic expectations:

**Single unprocessed frame:** Dark, noisy, flat, disappointing. You'll see the Milky Way but it looks nothing like the finished product.

**Stacked (10-20 frames):** Noticeably cleaner. The noise drops dramatically. Details start to emerge.

**Processed stack:** The Milky Way pops off the screen. Colors are rich. The foreground has detail and atmosphere. THIS is the image you envisioned.

The gap between a raw frame and a processed stack is enormous — and that's normal. Don't judge your photography by the raw files. Judge it by what you can create in processing.

## The Learning Curve

Honest talk: processing is a skill that takes months to develop. Your first edits will probably be over-saturated, over-sharpened, and heavy-handed. That's fine. Everyone goes through it. Keep a folder of your old edits and revisit them every few months — you'll be amazed at how much your eye develops.

Watch YouTube tutorials (Lonely Speck, Astro Backyard, Peter Zelinka are excellent), follow along with their workflows, and practice on your own images. Within a season, you'll develop a personal style and processing workflow that produces images you're genuinely proud of.
